text.title: Cohort Types and Retention Table Reading Guide
text.summary: Three cohort types (acquisition, behavioral, feature), what each reveals, and how to read a cohort retention table including the diagonal effect.

list.item: Acquisition cohort: group by signup period (week/month). Use for: tracking retention trends over time, measuring product quality improvement, comparing channel quality.
Behavioral cohort: group by action taken (e.g. completed onboarding vs skipped). Use for: finding valuable user behaviors to drive in onboarding, validating activation hypotheses.
Feature cohort: group by feature used in first N days. Use for: identifying "magic features" that predict retention, prioritizing feature adoption in onboarding flow.

rule.statement: Pair every acquisition cohort review with at least one behavioral cohort; acquisition cohorts show what, behavioral cohorts show why.
rule.rationale: Acquisition trends alone cannot identify what to fix; behavioral cohorts reveal the specific actions that separate high-retention from low-retention users.

section.title: Reading a Cohort Table
section.p: Table structure: row = cohort (signup period), column = day since joining, cell = % of cohort still active. Always show absolute cohort size alongside percentages.

list.item: reading rows: how a single cohort ages over time
reading columns: comparing the same time point across different cohorts (trend direction)
reading diagonals: moving diagonally down-left shows the current state — each diagonal cell represents what that cohort's users are doing "today"

rule.statement: Show the absolute cohort size next to every retention rate; a "30%" cell built on n=20 is anecdote, not data — grey-label it "underpowered".
rule.rationale: Small cohort percentages have high sampling variance; displaying only the percentage creates false confidence in unstable numbers.

example.note: Acquisition cohort: D30 improving from 12% (Oct) to 18% (Jan) → product quality or onboarding is improving. Investigate what changed in Nov-Jan to understand the cause.
Behavioral cohort: users completing onboarding (n=1500) have D30=45% vs skipped (n=2500) D30=15% → 3x lift. Priority: increase onboarding completion rate, not add features.
Feature cohort: users who invited a teammate in week 1 retain at D90=38% vs non-inviter D90=10% → "collaboration" is a magic feature. Priority: prompt team invite in onboarding.
